name: Portia
title: Arbiter
note: The chapter promises investigation and discovery - Nora says she'll "dig into past reports" and "review those reports tonight" - but when she actually does this research, there are no stakes attached to what she finds. She discovers "Samsquatch" reports from the 1970s and sees patterns of missing livestock and strange prints, but the draft never establishes what she's hoping to learn, what question this answers, or what decision this information allows her to make. The research scene becomes pure information delivery rather than a character pursuing a specific need, which deflates the tension the opening body-recovery built.

name: Horatio
title: Realist
note:
Danny’s line, “If anyone can crack this, it’s you,” comes too early in the chapter. We’ve just met Nora and seen her make one astute observation; this statement tries to shortcut the establishment of her competence and reputation rather than allowing her actions and demonstrated abilities to earn such high praise from a character we also barely know. A grounded reader needs to *see* her crack something before accepting this level of faith.

The jump from a potentially strange animal attack to "Samsquatch" and "unseen hand" reports is too swift and unearned. While Nora finds "reports of missing livestock, strange prints, trees stripped to the bone," the narrative merely tells us "a picture began to emerge" without showing the specific, compelling details that would make a skeptical reader, or Nora herself, seriously consider such a fantastical explanation. We are asked to accept the validity of this connection without sufficient tangible evidence presented to us.

name: Quixote
title: Visionary
note: The draft presents a compelling mystery and intriguing elements, but it plays it a bit too safe in the pivotal scene where Nora discovers the word "Samsquatch" in the archival reports. Instead of a mere mention of folklore, you could amplify the scene's strangeness by having Nora come across a vivid, cryptic drawing or a diary entry from the 1970s by a ranger claiming a sighting of something impossible. Perhaps this ranger described not just tracks but a bizarre encounter, voices in the forest, or an inexplicable event that defies rational explanation. This unexpected element would elevate the tension and deepen the enigma, inviting readers to question the nature of reality in the world you’re crafting.
